content: Major religious denominations involved in the Iran-Iraq War include Shia Islam in Iran, Sunni Islam in Iraq, and minority religions in Iran such as Zoroastrianism, Judaism, and Christianity, which supported the war effort. The conflict also had a lasting impact on the Sunni-Shia divide in the region. [Source: https://www.fletcherforum.org/home/2016/9/13/the-iran-iraq-war-and-sectarianism-in-the-middle-east, https://www.atlanticcouncil.org/blogs/iransource/lessons-from-the-iran-iraq-war-iranian-minorities-won-t-lead-transformative-change/, https://www.e-ir.info/2016/02/07/the-iran-iraq-war-the-use-of-religion-as-a-tool/]

The Shia-Sunni divide has complex historical roots. Shias constitute the majority in Iran, Iraq, Azerbaijan, and Bahrain, and a plurality in Lebanon [Source: https://www.cfr.org/article/sunni-shia-divide]. The Iranian Revolution of 1979 and the Iraq War of 2003 brought Sunni-Shia tensions into sharper focus [Source: https://worldhistoryedu.com/sunni-versus-shia-origin-story-of-the-divide/]. The Sunni-Shia divide has been intensified by the increasing politicization of Islam and the rise of fundamentalists, especially amid the Persian Gulf Wars, the U.S.-backed ouster of Saddam Hussein’s Sunni regime in Iraq, and the Arab Spring [Source: https://www.history.com/articles/sunni-shia-divide-islam-muslim].

During the Iran-Iraq War, religion significantly influenced social structures in both countries. In Iraq, the Ba'athist regime attempted to integrate Shia Muslims into the war effort and promoted Islam to counter Iranian religious fervor [Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Iran–Iraq_War]. The war was also fought on religious grounds, with sectarian divisions playing a key role [Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sectarian_violence_in_Iraq]. The strategic use of sectarianism during the war institutionalized sectarian identities, leading to conflicts over status, borders, and power [Source: https://studentreview.hks.harvard.edu/sectarianism-and-conflict-legacies-of-the-iran-iraq-war/].

The Iran-Iraq War had complex ethical considerations from a religious perspective.

*   **Justification of Violence:** While religious motivations didn't initiate the war, religion significantly motivated individuals to participate [Source: https://oxfordre.com/politics/abstract/10.1093/acrefore/9780190228637.001.0001/acrefore-9780190228637-e-812].
*   **Treatment of Prisoners:** Iran stated its treatment of Iraqi prisoners of war was based on humanitarian and Islamic considerations [Source: https://www.theiranproject.com/en/news/316627/iran痴-treatment-of-iraqi-pows-based-on-humanitarian-islamic-considerations]. However, there were also instances of executions of political prisoners [Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/1988_executions_of_Iranian_political_prisoners].
*   **Impact on Civilians:** Iraqi attacks on Iranian civilians contributed to Iran's agreement to a ceasefire [Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Iran–Iraq_War].

Globalization has a multifaceted impact on religious practices:

*   It can erode traditional beliefs as increased global interconnectedness exposes people to diverse perspectives [Source: https://moviecultists.com/how-globalization-affects-religious-practices-and-beliefs].
*   It fosters the emergence of new religious movements and hybrid spiritual practices [Source: https://newheightsboise.com/exploring-religious-traditions-and-meanings/].
*   Globalization's impact on religion is mixed, challenging the secularist view that religion will decline [Source: https://www.academia.edu/68309680/Globalization_Religion_and_Islam].

During the Iran-Iraq War, religious institutions played a significant role:

*   Religion motivated individuals to participate in the war [Source: https://oxfordre.com/politics/abstract/10.1093/acrefore/9780190228637.001.0001/acrefore-9780190228637-e-812].
*   Iranian political leaders used Islamic doctrines and symbolism to legitimize their wartime policies [Source: https://journals.uvic.ca/index.php/Illumine/article/view/7782/2574].
*   Saddam Hussein used religion as a tool to try to gain Iranian territory and regional power [Source: https://www.e-ir.info/pdf/61498].

Future trends in religious practices include:

*   Shifts in religion and secular life affecting communities, public services, and policy debates in the United States [Source: https://govfacts.org/long-term-challenges-future/social-cultural-futures/religious-secular-trends/]
*   Political religion's rebound, youth spiritual experimentation, AI in faith practices, interfaith collaborations, and global migration's faith impacts [Source: https://www.academicjobs.com/global-news/rise-in-religious-practices-2026-trends-statistics-reports-studies-194]
*   Believers increasingly customizing their religious practices [Source: https://bellenews.com/2025/03/19/arts-culture/understanding-religious-views-in-the-modern-day/]

During the Iran-Iraq War:

*   Each side strategically mixed religion and politics to garner internal allegiance and external support [Source: https://www.e-ir.info/2016/02/07/the-iran-iraq-war-the-use-of-religion-as-a-tool/].
*   The Iranian leadership painted the war as a battle between believers and unbelievers, effectively mobilizing support [Source: https://oxfordre.com/politics/abstract/10.1093/acrefore/9780190228637.001.0001/acrefore-9780190228637-e-812].
*   Religion played a crucial role in motivating individuals to participate in the war [Source: https://www.researchgate.net/publication/334440903_War_and_Religion_The_Iran-Iraq_War].
*   Despite a shared religious denominator, the war bred animosity between Iran and Iraq [Source: https://www.jstor.org/stable/41394115].

Key findings regarding the role of religion in the Iran-Iraq War:

*   **Denominations:** Shia Islam predominated in Iran, while Sunni Islam held sway in Iraq. Iranian minority religions also supported the war effort. The conflict intensified the Sunni-Shia divide [Source: https://www.e-ir.info/2016/02/07/the-iran-iraq-war-the-use-of-religion-as-a-tool/, https://www.fletcherforum.org/home/2016/9/13/the-iran-iraq-war-and-sectarianism-in-the-middle-east, https://www.atlanticcouncil.org/blogs/iransource/lessons-from-the-iran-iraq-war-iranian-minorities-won-t-lead-transformative-change/].
*   **Historical Development:** The Shia-Sunni divide has complex historical roots, intensified by the Iranian Revolution (1979) and the Iraq War (2003). The politicization of Islam and the rise of fundamentalism further exacerbated tensions [Source: https://www.cfr.org/article/sunni-shia-divide, https://worldhistoryedu.com/sunni-versus-shia-origin-story-of-the-divide/, https://www.history.com/articles/sunni-shia-divide-islam-muslim].
*   **Influence on Social Structures:** The Ba'athist regime in Iraq attempted to integrate Shia Muslims, while Iran saw energized Shi'ite fundamentalists. Sectarian divisions played a key role, and the strategic use of sectarianism institutionalized identities [Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Iran–Iraq_War, https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sectarian_violence_in_Iraq, https://studentreview.hks.harvard.edu/sectarianism-and-conflict-legacies-of-the-iran-iraq-war/].
*   **Ethical Considerations:** Religion motivated individuals to participate in the war, though it didn't initiate it. Iran stated its treatment of Iraqi prisoners of war was based on humanitarian and Islamic considerations [Source: https://oxfordre.com/politics/abstract/10.1093/acrefore/9780190228637.001.0001/acrefore-9780190228637-e-812, https://www.theiranproject.com/en/news/316627/iran%E2%80%99s-treatment-of-iraqi-pows-based-on-humanitarian-islamic-considerations].
*   **Impact of Globalization:** Globalization can erode traditional beliefs, foster new religious movements, and challenge the secularist view that religion will decline [Source: https://moviecultists.com/how-globalization-affects-religious-practices-and-beliefs, https://newheightsboise.com/exploring-religious-traditions-and-meanings/, https://www.academia.edu/68309680/Globalization_Religion_and_Islam].
*   **Role of Religious Institutions:** Religion motivated individuals, and political leaders used religious doctrines and symbolism to legitimize wartime policies [Source: https://oxfordre.com/politics/abstract/10.1093/acrefore/9780190228637.001.0001/acrefore-9780190228637-e-812, https://journals.uvic.ca/index.php/Illumine/article/view/7782/2574, https://www.e-ir.info/pdf/61498].
*   **Relationship with Other Domains**: Each side strategically mixed religion and politics to garner internal allegiance and external support [Source: https://www.e-ir.info/2016/02/07/the-iran-iraq-war-the-use-of-religion-as-a-tool/]. The Iranian leadership painted the war as a battle between believers and unbelievers, effectively mobilizing support [Source: https://oxfordre.com/politics/abstract/10.1093/acrefore/9780190228637.001.0001/acrefore-9780190228637-e-812].
